Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I'd have these intense bursts where I'd study for hours and hours, like 29 hours some weeks, but it wasn't organized at all. I'd often study late at night when my anxiety was keeping me awake anyway, or I'd cram everything into a few days before something was due. Being a hands-on learner, I tried to make things physical—writing notes by hand, pacing while reading—but the chaos of my schedule made it hard to stick to any real routine.
Q2: My pattern was completely dependent on deadlines and honestly, my mental state that day. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything because I was overwhelmed, and then I'd panic and try to catch up all at once. With 14 online courses to juggle and limited resources at home, I couldn't really afford the luxury of a consistent schedule. When anxiety hit hard, I'd sometimes freeze up entirely, and then I'd have to compensate later with marathon study sessions.
Q3: Usually panic, if I'm being honest. I'd log in when I suddenly remembered an assignment was due or when I got a notification that made my heart drop. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed something important, which happened a lot since my attendance was so low. The mindfulness exercises they introduced helped a little—I'd sometimes log in after doing breathing exercises to try and face what I'd been avoiding.
Q4: There were brief periods where I'd get into a rhythm, usually when I was feeling less anxious. I'd log in to watch videos because that felt less overwhelming than reading dense text. But those routines never lasted long—something would happen, I'd get stressed, miss a few days, and then logging in felt scary again because I knew I was behind. The routine would break and I'd have to start over.
Q5: The videos were honestly my lifeline—I watched over two hours worth because I could pause, rewind, and learn at my own pace without feeling judged. As a kinesthetic learner, I'd often act things out or take physical notes while watching. The peer interactions were helpful too when I could manage them; talking to other students made the material feel more real and less like this abstract thing I was failing at.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums—I only posted once the whole course. The idea of putting my thoughts out there for everyone to see made my anxiety spike. I also ignored a lot of the reading materials because staring at screens for long periods was exhausting, and I didn't always have reliable internet to keep accessing them. Some resources just felt like they were designed for people who had their lives together, not for someone barely keeping their head above water.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it connected to something I could physically do. Like, if I could apply a concept right away or practice something hands-on, I was in. But if it was just passive reading or something that felt disconnected from the exam, my brain would check out. With my limited time and energy, I had to be ruthless about what actually helped me pass.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. When you're barely completing 30% of the required stuff, optional feels like a joke. Required activities had this weight to them—they'd keep me up at night with worry. Optional ones I'd tell myself I'd get to 'later,' but later never came. There was also less guilt in skipping optional stuff, which was honestly a relief.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I'd download videos and PDFs when my internet was working well, then study them offline for hours. I filled notebooks with handwritten notes because writing helps me remember better than just reading on screen. I'd also look up YouTube videos explaining concepts differently, or find free resources online since I couldn't afford textbooks. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: So many times. I'd download a lecture, watch it three times while taking notes and pacing around my room, really wrestling with the material—but the LMS would only show I accessed it once. Or I'd study with a classmate in person, quizzing each other, which was super helpful for my kinesthetic learning style. None of that showed up anywhere. My 29 study hours definitely didn't all happen on the platform.
Q11: All the time, that was basically my strategy. I'd access something once, download it if possible, and then work with it offline for days. My internet isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab what I needed when I could. I'd print things at the library when I had money for printing, then study from paper. The LMS probably thought I barely engaged with some materials when really I'd worn them out offline.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. My anxiety would get so bad that even opening the LMS felt impossible—my hands would shake and I'd feel this overwhelming dread. I missed a lot of classes during those times, which explains my attendance. Family stuff at home was stressful too, and with limited resources, sometimes I had to prioritize work over school just to help out.
Q13: Constantly. The workload across 14 courses was insane, and I'd get so fatigued that I couldn't process anything. Confusion made it worse—when I didn't understand something, my anxiety would tell me I was stupid and shouldn't even try. I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen, too overwhelmed to actually do anything. The mindfulness intervention helped a bit, but it couldn't fix the underlying chaos of my life.
Q14: Yeah, more times than I want to admit. I'd have the LMS open, maybe even a video playing, but I'd be completely dissociated—just going through the motions while my mind was somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I was too anxious to actually absorb anything, so I'd 'watch' a whole lecture and remember nothing. My engagement numbers probably look better than my actual learning in those moments.
Q15: I avoided assignments all the time, which is why my completion rate was so low. The bigger or more important something seemed, the more terrifying it was to start. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, then tomorrow would come and I'd be even more behind and more scared. It's this awful cycle where avoidance creates more anxiety which creates more avoidance. I knew it was self-destructive but I couldn't stop.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd ignore everything until the last possible moment, then have these frantic sessions where I'd try to do everything at once. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and flatlined otherwise. It wasn't healthy—I'd be up all night, anxious and exhausted—but fear of failing was the only thing stronger than my fear of engaging.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. When it was positive, it surprised me and gave me a brief boost. But critical feedback, even constructive stuff, would send me spiraling. I'd avoid checking my grades for days because I was terrified of what I'd see. Sometimes good feedback motivated me to engage more, but bad feedback made me want to disappear. My low resilience made it hard to bounce back from criticism.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so much content and so many deadlines across my 14 courses. When things were clearly organized and broken into small pieces, I could manage better. But when modules were dense or expectations were unclear, I'd shut down. The flexibility of online learning was both good and bad—good because I could work around my anxiety, bad because there was no external structure forcing me to stay on track.
Q19: Video content made engagement easier because I could control the pace and replay confusing parts. Clear, simple instructions helped too. What made it harder was when there were too many steps to complete something, or when I had to post publicly in discussions. Anything that felt high-stakes or exposed triggered my anxiety. Also, not having great resources at home meant I couldn't always access things when I needed to.
Q20: That's literally me. I'd log in constantly, watch videos, click through materials—but then freeze when it came to actually submitting work. The submission part felt so final and judgmental. Browsing and watching felt safe; putting my work out there to be graded felt terrifying. Sometimes I'd even complete assignments but not submit them because I was convinced they weren't good enough. High activity, low output—that's anxiety in action.
Q21: I think some students are just really efficient, or they study in ways that don't show up online. Maybe they have good textbooks, study groups, or they just learn fast. For me, I did a lot offline, but my performance was still just okay because the anxiety interfered with exams. But yeah, someone could totally learn everything they need outside the LMS and just show up for assessments. The platform isn't the only place learning happens.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on things—the surface level stuff. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just staring blankly at a screen. They miss offline studying, emotional state, life circumstances, everything that makes you a human and not just a data point. My logs probably look chaotic, but there's a whole story they can't tell.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about the material and putting real mental effort into understanding it, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, connecting ideas to real life. It's not about how many times I log in or how many minutes I spend—it's about whether my brain is actually working on the content. Some of my most engaged moments happened with a notebook in my room, nowhere near a computer.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was fighting just to show up at all. It can't capture the panic attacks before logging in, or the relief when I finally understood something after struggling for hours offline. It doesn't know about my family situation, my limited resources, or how the mindfulness exercises actually helped me get through some dark moments. My data looks like a struggling student, but it doesn't show someone who was genuinely trying their best under really difficult circumstances.
